refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the acceptance criterion (≥0.999), specifies the measurement scope (bidirectional link), and defines the evaluation period. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to equipment immobilization or unsafe states.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during the evaluation period; calculate availability as (total_time_link_up) / (total_evaluation_time). Acceptance: measured availability ≥ customer-defined target (default 0.999). Verification method: automated telemetry logging and post-evaluation statistical analysis.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall initiate safe state transition upon link loss.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall maintain redundant communication paths to achieve target availability.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link state transitions for availability audit.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Single communication link with best-effort reliability (no redundancy)
DP-B: Dual independent communication paths (primary + secondary failover) with automatic switchover logic
DP-C: Mesh network topology with multi-hop redundancy and dynamic routing
DP-D: Hybrid approach: primary cellular link + secondary satellite/radio backup with intelligent link selection
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Hybrid dual-path architecture with primary cellular (4G/5G) and secondary satellite/radio backup link, coupled with intelligent link selection logic that monitors both paths and switches to secondary upon primary degradation, ensuring availability ≥0.999 through path diversity and automatic failover.

potential_sources: Link selection logic must not couple tightly to specific network provider APIs (risk: vendor lock-in).
Failover timing must be decoupled from application-layer command processing (risk: race conditions during switchover).
Telemetry buffering during switchover must not couple to vehicle control state machine (risk: stale data causing unsafe commands).
coupling_check.notes: DP-D exhibits acceptable decoupling: primary/secondary paths are logically independent; failover is managed by a dedicated link management layer; telemetry and tasking flows remain independent. Coupling risks are manageable through clear interface contracts and state synchronization protocols.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Cellular and satellite/radio coverage is available at the worksite; if not, availability target may be unachievable.
ASSUMPTION: Dual-path infrastructure cost is acceptable to the customer.
RISK: Satellite link latency may exceed operator tolerance; mitigation: pre-evaluate latency budget and operator training.
RISK: Failover logic bugs could cause link thrashing (rapid switching); mitigation: hysteresis and link stability monitoring.
RISK: Network provider outages could affect both paths if they share backbone; mitigation: select providers with geographically diverse infrastructure.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) failover decision criteria (e.g., packet loss threshold, latency threshold), (2) minimum link stability window before switchover, (3) telemetry synchronization protocol during transition, and (4) fallback safe state if both paths fail. This will reduce ambiguity and support detailed design.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the dual-path communication architecture, link monitoring, failover logic, and telemetry/tasking flow management. It interfaces with the Vehicle module (via Tasking and Telemetry) and Safety Services (via SafetyAlert). It manages the Network Link abstraction (primary cellular + secondary satellite/radio).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands from tele-operator; routed through Teleoperation module to Vehicle. Teleoperation ensures commands are only sent when link is available and healthy.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor data) from Vehicle through Teleoperation module to tele-operator. Teleoperation buffers and prioritizes telemetry during link transitions.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals link loss or degradation events to Safety Services, triggering safe state transitions (e.g., equipment immobilization). Teleoperation detects link faults and initiates SafetyAlert.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Abstraction representing dual communication paths (primary cellular + secondary satellite/radio). Teleoperation manages link selection, monitoring, and failover at this interface.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic, failover, and dual-path management. All DP-D components (link selection, failover, monitoring) are cohesive and belong in this module. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert signaling crosses to Safety Services (expected and necessary).

Vehicle module owns vehicle control and state; it should NOT own communication link management. Assigning DP-D to Vehicle would scatter link logic across vehicle firmware, creating tight coupling and diffusion. Not recommended.

Safety Services owns safe state policies and enforcement. While it consumes SafetyAlert from Teleoperation, it should not own the link monitoring/failover logic itself. Assigning DP-D here would blur the boundary between communication reliability and safety policy. Not recommended.
NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Assigning DP-D here would bypass the Teleoperation module abstraction and create diffusion across multiple sub-systems. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-D (dual-path link availability architecture). It has high cohesion (all link management logic is contained), low diffusion (only SafetyAlert crosses boundary as expected), and clear ownership of the Network Link abstraction. This assignment aligns with the principle of separation of concerns: Teleoperation handles communication reliability; Vehicle handles control; Safety Services handles safe state enforcement.
containment_risks: RISK: If Teleoperation module becomes too large (link management + failover + telemetry buffering + tasking routing), it may require further decomposition. Mitigation: plan for sub-module structure (e.g., LinkManager, FailoverController, TelemetryBuffer) within Teleoperation.

RISK: Tight coupling between failover logic and Vehicle state machine could emerge if not carefully managed. Mitigation: enforce strict interface contracts (Tasking/Telemetry) and avoid direct Vehicle state inspection in Teleoperation.
RISK: SafetyAlert interface may become a bottleneck if multiple link fault scenarios trigger alerts. Mitigation: design SafetyAlert as a priority queue and define alert severity levels.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly define the Teleoperation module's internal structure: (1) LinkManager sub-component (monitors primary/secondary paths, decides failover), (2) FailoverController sub-component (executes switchover, manages hysteresis), (3) TelemetryBuffer sub-component (buffers/prioritizes telemetry during transitions), (4) TaskingRouter sub-component (routes operator commands only when link is healthy). This will reduce internal diffusion and support detailed design.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid dual-path with intelligent failover) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve ≥0.999 link availability). The DP provides a clear solution path: dual independent communication paths (cellular + satellite/radio) with automatic failover logic ensure that if one path fails, the other maintains the link. This is a sound design principle for achieving high availability. No decoupling issues identified. DP-D is implementable and measurable.

diffusion_risks: Minimal diffusion: Teleoperation module owns DP-D with containment_score=0.95. Only SafetyAlert crosses boundary (expected and necessary for safety integration).
No diffusion to Vehicle or NGHE Solution top-level. Link management logic is cleanly encapsulated.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation is the correct architectural home for DP-D. The module boundary aligns with the functional scope (communication reliability) and avoids scattering link logic across vehicle firmware or safety policies. Ownership is clear and defensible.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: FR-NGHE-010 is verifiable: link availability can be measured via continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during the evaluation period. Teleoperation module can log link transitions and compute availability as (time_up) / (total_time). Acceptance criterion (≥0.999) is quantitative and testable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module is responsible for achieving ≥0.999 link availability through DP-D (dual-path failover). Vehicle module is responsible for safe state transitions upon SafetyAlert. Safety Services is responsible for enforcing safe state policies. No ambiguity in responsibility.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D specification to include: (1) failover decision criteria (packet loss %, latency threshold), (2) link stability window (minimum time before switchover), (3) telemetry synchronization protocol during transition, (4) fallback safe state if both paths fail.
Define Teleoperation module's internal sub-component structure (LinkManager, FailoverController, TelemetryBuffer, TaskingRouter) to reduce internal diffusion and support detailed design.
Establish acceptance test plan: specify evaluation period duration, measurement methodology, and statistical confidence level for availability calculation.
Document assumptions: cellular/satellite coverage availability, dual-path infrastructure cost acceptance, operator latency tolerance, network provider diversity.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (link loss detection, safe state transition, redundant paths, audit logging). DP-D is sound and well-aligned with the refined FR. Teleoperation is the correct IM owner with high containment and low diffusion. All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass. The next step is to decompose the sub-FRs and refine the DP specification to support detailed design. No blocking issues; proceed to the next decomposition level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall initiate safe state transition upon link loss.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall maintain redundant communication paths to achieve target availability.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link state transitions for availability audit.

assumptions: Cellular (4G/5G) and satellite/radio coverage is available at the worksite.
Dual-path infrastructure cost is acceptable to the customer.
Operator can tolerate satellite link latency (typically 500ms–1s).
Network providers have geographically diverse backbone infrastructure to avoid correlated failures.
Link availability is measured as uptime ratio over a defined evaluation period (e.g., 30 days).
Safe state (equipment immobilization) is acceptable during link loss events.
open_risks: Failover logic bugs could cause link thrashing (rapid switching); mitigation: implement hysteresis and link stability monitoring.
Satellite link latency may exceed operator tolerance; mitigation: pre-evaluate latency budget and conduct operator training.
Network provider outages could affect both paths if they share backbone; mitigation: select providers with geographically diverse infrastructure.
Telemetry buffering during switchover could cause stale data; mitigation: implement timestamp-based data freshness checks.
SafetyAlert interface could become a bottleneck; mitigation: design as priority queue with severity levels.
evidence_needed: Cellular and satellite coverage maps for target worksites.
Network provider SLA documentation and backbone diversity analysis.
Failover logic simulation and stress testing (link thrashing scenarios).
Telemetry synchronization protocol specification and validation.
Safe state transition procedure and vehicle firmware integration plan.
Link state logging and availability calculation methodology.
Acceptance test plan with evaluation period, measurement methodology, and statistical confidence level.
